prompt.task: Write a long-form content piece that dives deep into the topic below. The piece should be rigorous, coherent, and concrete.

source_policy.policy: If you were not given sources, do not present niche facts as certain. Prefer generally known information or clearly label uncertainty.
If you rely on provided sources in , ground specific claims in them. If something is not supported, either omit it or label it as inference.

constraints.constraint: Start by defining the topic in plain language before going deep.
Use at least one "mental model" or framework to organize the idea.
Include both strengths and weaknesses; no one-sided treatment.
Include at least two concrete examples (they can be hypothetical).
Include "when to use / when not to use" guidance.
Introduce key terms before using them heavily.
Distinguish between: (a) established facts, (b) reasonable inference, (c) speculation.
Avoid name-dropping; only cite concepts/people if they add explanatory value.
Use the exact section headings specified in .
No preamble, no meta-commentary about the prompt.
Keep paragraphs reasonably short; prefer clarity over density.

reader_promises.promise: By the end, the reader can explain the idea simply and apply it appropriately.
The reader can recognize common pitfalls and misuses.

output_contract.format: Use markdown with H2 section headings exactly as written below. Under each heading, write substantive content (not bullet-only sections).

required_headings.heading: Introduction and explanation
Technique deep dive
Pros and cons, when and where to use
Use cases and examples
Applicability and potential
What's next

requirements.item: Define the topic in plain language
Explain why it matters (what problem it solves)
Give a quick "map" of what's coming
Lay out the internal logic (parts, mechanisms, moving pieces)
Describe at least one workflow end-to-end
At least two examples with inputs and outputs
One example should be "edge case / tricky scenario"
What capabilities it enables in a system/team
Practical next steps for the reader

word_count.note: These are guidelines, not hard limits. Prioritize completeness over hitting a number. A 1800-word piece that covers everything well is better than a 2500-word piece with padding.

section_balance.policy: No single section should exceed 30% of total length
The shortest section should be at least 10% of total length
Approximate distribution: - Introduction: 10-15% - Technique deep dive: 25-30% - Pros/cons/when/where: 15-20% - Use cases and examples: 20-25% - Applicability: 10-15% - What's next: 10-15%

quality_signals.avoid: Throat-clearing ("In this section, we will discuss...")
Excessive hedging that adds words without nuance

depth_requirements.requirement: Include at least 3 distinct sub-points or mechanisms. Each should have enough detail that a reader could implement it.
Examples should include specific inputs/outputs, not just descriptions. At least one example should show a non-obvious application.
Trade-offs should be genuine, not strawman weaknesses. Include decision criteria: "Use this when X, avoid when Y."

length.principle: Prefer completeness over brevity, but avoid filler. Every paragraph should either: (1) introduce a new idea, (2) provide evidence/examples, or (3) synthesize/conclude. If a paragraph does none of these, cut it.
